subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Volume of Solids
stimulus:
A tank has a height of 10 feet. The area of the horizontal cross section of the tank at height $h$ feet is given by the function $A$, where $A(h)$ is measured in square feet. The table below gives values of $A(h)$ for selected heights $h$.

| $h$ (feet) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A(h)$ (square feet) | 50.3 | 14.4 | 6.5 | 2.9 |

parts.prompt: Use a left Riemann sum with the three subintervals indicated by the data in the table to approximate the volume of the tank. Indicate units of measure.

scoring_points.description: Correct left Riemann sum setup using the three subintervals $[0,2]$, $[2,5]$, and $[5,10]$
Correct answer with units of cubic feet

parts.prompt: Does the approximation in part (a) overestimate or underestimate the volume of the tank? Explain your reasoning.

scoring_points.description: States overestimate with correct reasoning that $A$ is a decreasing function, so the left Riemann sum uses values of $A$ that are greater than or equal to the actual values on each subinterval

required_elements: $A$ is decreasing (or each left endpoint value is greater than the values of $A$ on the subinterval)

scoring_points.correct_example: The approximation is an overestimate because $A$ is a decreasing function. On each subinterval the left endpoint value of $A$ is greater than or equal to all other values of $A$ on that subinterval, so the left Riemann sum is greater than the actual volume.

parts.prompt: The area, in square feet, of the horizontal cross section at height $h$ feet is modeled by the function $f$ given by $$f(h) = \frac{50.3}{e^{0.2h} + h}.$$ Based on this model, find the volume of the tank. Indicate units of measure.

scoring_points.description: Correct integral setup: volume equals $\int_0^{10} f(h)\,dh$

parts.prompt: Water is pumped into the tank. When the height of the water is 5 feet, the height is increasing at the rate of $0.26$ foot per minute. Using the model from part (c), find the rate at which the volume of water is changing with respect to time when the height of the water is 5 feet. Indicate units of measure.

scoring_points.description: Correct numerical answer with units of cubic feet per minute
